title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text: vignette illustrates main use slicer package means toy example. Empirical distributions generated bivariate Gaussian mixtures four isotropic components laid symmetrically around origin. distributions differ one another rotated counterclockwise angle ω∈[0,π/2)\omega \[0, \pi/2). goal analysis retrieve specific angles distributions rotated.

title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text: Let’s load slicer. generate input distributions, define generate_data(): plots show two empirical distributions, using rotation angle ω=π/16\omega = \pi/16 using one ω=3π/8\omega = 3\pi/8.  Finally, function computing root mean squared error predictions:
code: library(slicer) generate_data <- function(n = 200, a = 5, angle = 0, Sigma = diag(1, 2)) {   mus <- cbind(c(a, 0), c(0, a), c(-a, 0), c(0, -a))   R <- rbind(     c(cos(angle), -sin(angle)),     c(sin(angle), cos(angle))   )   cluster <- sample(1:4, size = n, replace = TRUE)   d <- matrix(0, nrow = n, ncol = 2)   for (i in 1:4) {     d[which(cluster == i), ] <- MASS::mvrnorm(       sum(cluster == i), mu = mus[, i], Sigma = Sigma     )   }   d %*% t(R) } set.seed(2026) # for reproducibility  op <- par(no.readonly = TRUE) par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) generate_data(angle = pi/16) |>    plot(xlab = "first dimension", ylab = "second dimension", asp = 1) generate_data(angle = 3*pi/8) |>    plot(xlab = "first dimension", ylab = "second dimension", asp = 1) par(op) rmse <- function(predictions, true_values) {   sqrt(mean((predictions - true_values)^2)) }

title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text: generate 60 matrices (40 training, 20 testing) containing empirical distributions rotation angles sampled uniformly [0,π/4)[0, \pi/4). empirical distribution based 200 data points.
code: sample_size <- 200 N_train <- 40 N_test  <- 20 angle_range <- c(0, pi/4)  angles <- runif(N_train + N_test, angle_range[1], angle_range[2])  distributions <- vector("list", N_train + N_test) for (i in seq_len(N_train + N_test)) {   distributions[[i]] <- generate_data(angle = angles[i]) }

title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text: can use compute_all_distances() compute pairwise distances input distributions. supply optional vector test_idx test set indices, pairwise distance computations test objects (needed) skipped. compute sliced Wasserstein distances, need first generate θ1,…,θL\theta_1, \dots, \theta_L sampled uniformly random unit sphere; generate_directions() takes care . ’ll use L=25L = 25 set d=2d = 2 since ’re working two dimensions. ’re interested estimated sliced Wasserstein distances, can set keep_projections FALSE. 60×6060 \times 60 matrix sw_distances contains squared (estimated) sliced Wasserstein distances among forty training objects training objects twenty test objects. pairwise distances among test objects set NA. can also compute (normal) Wasserstein distances distributions projected along certain dimensions. instances, compute pairwise Wasserstein distances along first margin along second margin, set projection directions e1=(1,0)⊤e_1 = (1, 0)^{\top} e2=(0,1)⊤e_2 = (0, 1)^{\top}. also set keep_projections = TRUE, cause function output list two matrices squared Wasserstein distances: one margin.
code: thetas <- generate_directions(L = 25, d = 2) sw_distances <- compute_all_distances(distributions, thetas, verbose = FALSE,     keep_projections = FALSE, test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test)) marginal_distances <- compute_all_distances(distributions, diag(1, 2),      verbose = FALSE, keep_projections = TRUE,      test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test)) str(marginal_distances) #> List of 2 #>  $ : num [1:60, 1:60] 0 1.274 0.931 0.548 0.649 ... #>  $ : num [1:60, 1:60] 0 2.177 0.121 0.445 0.599 ...

what: Gaussian process models with tuned hyperparameters
title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text: function fit_gpr() takes single matrix squared pairwise distances uses input Gaussian process regression model Gaussian RBF kernel. model’s kernel’s hyperparameters tuned using training data minimising negative marginal log-likelihood. output consists predictions test data, root mean squared error predictions (true test outcomes provided), estimated hyperparameters.  function fit_gpr() can also used multiple matrices squared distances provided. Now, estimated length-scale (kernel) variance hyperparameters provided Gaussian RBF corresponding squared distance matrix.  can combine marginal sliced Wasserstein distances list three distance matrices, :  Parallel processing can enabled using cores parameter:
code: sw_fit <- fit_gpr(sw_distances,   training_idx = seq_len(N_train),   test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test),   y_train = angles[seq_len(N_train)],    verbose = TRUE) #> Hyperparameter search 1 of 10. #> Optimum set at -58.8175077. #> Hyperparameter search 2 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 3 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 4 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 5 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 6 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 7 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 8 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -58.8175077 to -58.8176617. #> Hyperparameter search 9 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 10 of 10. str(sw_fit) #> List of 6 #>  $ test_predictions: num [1:20] 0.241 0.7091 0.2402 0.5074 0.0604 ... #>  $ RMSE            : logi NA #>  $ length_scale    : num 1.57 #>  $ scaling_factor  : num 0.0758 #>  $ noise_variance  : num 1.57e-07 #>  $ nll             : num -58.8 plot(angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)], sw_fit$test_predictions,      xlab = "true test outcomes", ylab = "predicted test outcomes", asp = 1) rmse(sw_fit$test_predictions, angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)]) #> [1] 0.03804247 marginal_fit <- fit_gpr(marginal_distances,   training_idx = seq_len(N_train),   test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test),   y_train = angles[seq_len(N_train)],    verbose = TRUE) #> Hyperparameter search 1 of 10. #> Optimum set at -48.3151392. #> Hyperparameter search 2 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -48.3151392 to -61.5134707. #> Hyperparameter search 3 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 4 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 5 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 6 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 7 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 8 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -61.5134707 to -61.5134707. #> Hyperparameter search 9 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 10 of 10. str(marginal_fit) #> List of 6 #>  $ test_predictions: num [1:20] 0.2395 0.6678 0.2429 0.5619 0.0919 ... #>  $ RMSE            : logi NA #>  $ length_scale    : num [1:2] 3.9 6.09 #>  $ scaling_factor  : num [1:2] 0.347 0.146 #>  $ noise_variance  : num 0.000448 #>  $ nll             : num -61.5 plot(angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)], marginal_fit$test_predictions,      xlab = "true test outcomes", ylab = "predicted test outcomes", asp = 1) rmse(marginal_fit$test_predictions, angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)]) #> [1] 0.05219439 total_fit <- fit_gpr(list(sw_distances, marginal_distances[[1]], marginal_distances[[2]]),   training_idx = seq_len(N_train),   test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test),   y_train = angles[seq_len(N_train)],    verbose = TRUE) #> Hyperparameter search 1 of 10. #> Optimum set at -58.8320454. #> Hyperparameter search 2 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -58.8320454 to -61.5129334. #> Hyperparameter search 3 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -61.5129334 to -63.080251. #> Hyperparameter search 4 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 5 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 6 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 7 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 8 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 9 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 10 of 10. str(total_fit) #> List of 6 #>  $ test_predictions: num [1:20] 0.247 0.69 0.247 0.549 0.085 ... #>  $ RMSE            : logi NA #>  $ length_scale    : num [1:3] 0.421 3.833 5.482 #>  $ scaling_factor  : num [1:3] 0.00118 0.24703 0.12446 #>  $ noise_variance  : num 3.32e-08 #>  $ nll             : num -63.1 plot(angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)], total_fit$test_predictions,      xlab = "true test outcomes", ylab = "predicted test outcomes", asp = 1) rmse(total_fit$test_predictions, angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)]) #> [1] 0.04697571 total_fit <- fit_gpr(list(sw_distances, marginal_distances[[1]], marginal_distances[[2]]),   training_idx = seq_len(N_train),   test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test),   y_train = angles[seq_len(N_train)], runs = 50L, cores = 2L) str(total_fit) #> List of 6 #>  $ test_predictions: num [1:20] 0.268 0.682 0.25 0.581 0.11 ... #>  $ RMSE            : logi NA #>  $ length_scale    : num [1:3] 0.283 4.653 182.597 #>  $ scaling_factor  : num [1:3] 6.77e-04 7.52e-01 7.39e-15 #>  $ noise_variance  : num 4.2e-10 #>  $ nll             : num -61.8

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distances for Regression
text: slicer provides functions computing sliced Wasserstein distances well one-dimensional Wasserstein distances empirical distributions represented matrices. distances can fed kernel function (currently, Gaussian radial basis function kernel supported), can used Gaussian process regression model.

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distances for Regression
text: can install development version slicer GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("janhove/slicer")

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distances for Regression
text: Please refer vignette brief tutorial.

what: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
text: function computes squared 2-Wasserstein distances projections several empirical directions along specified projection directions. projection directions can optionally transformed means linear map. functions can output squared distances along projection direction, squared distances averaged projection directions.

title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
code: compute_all_distances(   distributions,   thetas,   A = NULL,   verbose = TRUE,   keep_projections = TRUE,   test_idx = NULL )

title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
text: distributions list matrices representing empirical distributions. thetas matrix, row represents projection direction. Optionally, matrix used transform projection direction. verbose TRUE, show progress. keep_projections TRUE, distance matrix projection direction output. FALSE, distance matrices different projection directions averaged. test_idx Optionally, vector indices. supplied, skip distance computations distribution pairs whose indices occur vector.

title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
text: keep_projections = TRUE, list squared-distance matrices, one projection direction; otherwise, matrix averaged squared distances.

title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
code: M1 <- matrix(rnorm(50), ncol = 5) M2 <- matrix(rnorm(50), ncol = 5) M3 <- matrix(rnorm(250), ncol = 5) # Sliced Wasserstein: my_directions <- generate_directions(20, 5) compute_all_distances(list(M1, M2, M3), my_directions,   keep_projections = FALSE, verbose = FALSE) #>           [,1]      [,2]      [,3] #> [1,] 0.0000000 0.3726307 0.2292539 #> [2,] 0.3726307 0.0000000 0.2697058 #> [3,] 0.2292539 0.2697058 0.0000000 # Marginal Wasserstein distances: marginal_wass <- compute_all_distances(list(M1, M2, M3), diag(1, 5),   keep_projections = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) marginal_wass[[3]] # along third dimension #>            [,1]      [,2]       [,3] #> [1,] 0.00000000 0.4029302 0.09973663 #> [2,] 0.40293015 0.0000000 0.35042965 #> [3,] 0.09973663 0.3504296 0.00000000 # Reweight projection directions A <- diag(c(4, 0.5, 3, 2, 1)) shear_wass <- compute_all_distances(list(M1, M2, M3), diag(1, 5), A = A,   keep_projections = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) shear_wass[[3]] #>           [,1]     [,2]      [,3] #> [1,] 0.0000000 3.626371 0.8976297 #> [2,] 3.6263714 0.000000 3.1538668 #> [3,] 0.8976297 3.153867 0.0000000

what: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
text: function generates predictions using Gaussian process model one several Gaussian RBF kernels. hyperparameters tuned using training data minimising model's negative marginal log-likelihood.

title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
code: fit_gpr(   D2,   training_idx,   test_idx,   y_train,   y_test = NULL,   centre = TRUE,   use_gradient = TRUE,   runs = 10L,   cores = 1L,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
text: D2 matrix, list matrices, containing squared pairwise distances objects. training_idx vector row (column) numbers corresponding training entries D2. test_idx vector row (column) numbers corresponding test entries D2. y_train vector training outcomes. y_test Optionally, vector test outcomes. centre TRUE, training outcomes centred around mean fitting model. mean added back predictions end. use_gradient TRUE, closed-form expressions RBF's gradient used. Else, optimiser uses finite-differences method. runs Number independent attempts find minimum optimising hyperparameters. cores Number cores used parallel processing. verbose TRUE, progress shown console. works cores == 1L.

title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
text: list containing predictions test objects, root mean squared error (true test outcomes provided), tuned hyperparameter values, negative log marginal likelihood (nll).

title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
code: # Multiple kernels N1 <- 25 N2 <- 10 x_train1 <- runif(N1, -pi, pi) x_train2 <- runif(N1, 0, 1) x_test1  <- runif(N2, -pi, pi) x_test2  <- runif(N2, 0, 1) y_train <- x_train2 * plogis(x_train1) * cos(x_train1) y_test <- x_test2 * plogis(x_test1) * cos(x_test1) D2_1 <- outer(c(x_train1, x_test1), c(x_train1, x_test1), "-")^2 D2_2 <- outer(c(x_train2, x_test2), c(x_train2, x_test2), "-")^2 # Single core fit <- fit_gpr(list(D2_1, D2_2), seq_len(N1), N1 + seq_len(N2),   y_train, y_test, runs = 50) #> Hyperparameter search 1 of 50. #> Optimum set at -6.7313219. #> Hyperparameter search 2 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 3 of 50. #> Current optimum improved from -6.7313219 to -9.0170496. #> Hyperparameter search 4 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 5 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 6 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 7 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 8 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 9 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 10 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 11 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 12 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 13 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 14 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 15 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 16 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 17 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 18 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 19 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 20 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 21 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 22 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 23 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 24 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 25 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 26 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 27 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 28 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 29 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 30 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 31 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 32 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 33 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 34 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 35 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 36 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 37 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 38 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 39 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 40 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 41 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 42 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 43 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 44 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 45 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 46 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 47 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 48 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 49 of 50. #> Hyperparameter search 50 of 50. plot(fit$test_predictions, y_test)  fit #> $test_predictions #>  [1]  0.103819650 -0.132069036 -0.003162020  0.001227841  0.005412415 #>  [6]  0.006466601 -0.231317504 -0.120333455 -0.010845450 -0.041972268 #>  #> $RMSE #> [1] 0.1799737 #>  #> $length_scale #> [1] 1.24597125 0.05817039 #>  #> $scaling_factor #> [1] 0.03429806 0.01700076 #>  #> $noise_variance #> [1] 0.01004947 #>  #> $nll #> [1] -9.01705 #>  # Multiple cores fit <- fit_gpr(list(D2_1, D2_2), seq_len(N1), N1 + seq_len(N2),   y_train, y_test, runs = 50, cores = 2) fit #> $test_predictions #>  [1]  0.103819637 -0.132069045 -0.003162046  0.001227850  0.005412481 #>  [6]  0.006466561 -0.231317507 -0.120333474 -0.010845472 -0.041972372 #>  #> $RMSE #> [1] 0.1799737 #>  #> $length_scale #> [1] 1.2459726 0.0581704 #>  #> $scaling_factor #> [1] 0.03429812 0.01700075 #>  #> $noise_variance #> [1] 0.01004947 #>  #> $nll #> [1] -9.01705 #>   # Single kernel N1 <- 25 N2 <- 10 x_train <- seq(-pi, pi, length.out = N1) x_test  <- runif(N2, -pi, pi) y_train <- x_train * plogis(x_train) * cos(x_train) y_test <- x_test * plogis(x_test) * cos(x_test) D2 <- outer(c(x_train, x_test), c(x_train, x_test), "-")^2 fit <- fit_gpr(D2, seq_len(N1), N1 + seq_len(N2), y_train, y_test,   runs = 50L, cores = 2) curve(x * plogis(x) * cos(x), -pi, pi) points(x_train, y_train, pch = 1) points(x_test, fit$test_predictions, pch = 16)  fit$RMSE #> [1] 1.556357e-07

title: Generate random projection directions — generate_directions
text: Obtain L random vectors unit sphere d-dimensional Euclidean space.

title: Generate random projection directions — generate_directions
code: generate_directions(L, d)

title: Generate random projection directions — generate_directions
text: L Number projection directions. d Dimension.

title: Generate random projection directions — generate_directions
text: L d matrix, row contains unit vector.

title: Generate random projection directions — generate_directions

what: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
text: Computes posterior mean predictions Gaussian process regression model given precomputed kernel matrices. implementation follows Algorithm 2.1 Rasmussen Williams (2006), optional centring step automatic jitter escalation handle near-singular kernel matrices.

title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
code: gpr_predict(Kxx, Kxstar, y_train, centre = TRUE, lambda2 = 1e-06)

title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
text: Kxx Kernel matrix evaluated training inputs. Kxstar Matrix kernel values test training inputs. row corresponds one test input. y_train Vector training outcomes. centre TRUE, training outcomes centred around mean. lambda2 Kernel noise variance.

title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
text: vector posterior mean predictions test inputs.

title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
code: N1 <- 25 N2 <- 10 x_train <- seq(-pi, pi, length.out = N1) x_test  <- runif(N2, -pi, pi) distance <- outer(c(x_train, x_test), c(x_train, x_test), "-") |> abs() kernel <- rbf(distance, length_scale = 1, variance = 1) Kxx    <- kernel[1:N1, 1:N1] Kxstar <- kernel[(N1+1):(N1+N2), 1:N1] y_train <- ifelse(x_train == 0, 2*pi, sin(2*pi*x_train) / x_train) curve(sin(2*pi*x)/x, from = -pi, to = pi) points(x_train, y_train) points(x_test, gpr_predict(Kxx, Kxstar, y_train), pch = 16)

what: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
text: Applies north-west corner algorithm obtain p-Wasserstein distance two one-dimensional empirical distributions.

title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
code: nw_corner_distance(   x,   y,   presorted = FALSE,   p = 2,   eps = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps) )

title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
text: x, y Vectors representing one-dimensional empirical distributions. presorted Set TRUE x y sorted obtain speed-. p Order Wasserstein distance. eps Numerical precision floating point comparisons.

title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
text: p-Wasserstein distance x y.

title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
code: x <- rnorm(10) y <- rnorm(40) nw_corner_distance(x, y) #> [1] 0.6348845 nw_corner_distance(sort(x), sort(y), presorted = TRUE) #> [1] 0.6348845

what: Gaussian radial basis function kernel — rbf

text: kernel matrix dimensions D.

code: rbf_multiple(D_list, length_scales, variances, squared = TRUE)

text: D_list list matrices pairwise (possibly squared) distances. length_scales vector length-scale parameters. variances vector kernel variance parameters. squared TRUE (default), distance matrix assumed contain squared distances. FALSE, distance matrix assumed contain raw distances.

what: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
text: Estimate sliced Wasserstein distance two empirical distributions represented matrices using Monte Carlo simulation. Optionally, matrix thetas can supplied. row thetas interpreted projection direction. rows unit vectors, allowing users transform projection directions rather matrices.

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
code: sliced_wasserstein(x, y, p = 2, thetas = NULL, L = 50, seed = NULL)

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
text: x, y Matrices representing empirical distributions. p Order sliced Wasserstein distance. thetas Optionally, matrix, row represents projection direction. L thetas provided, L random projection directions generated. seed Optional random seed.

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
text: Estimated sliced Wasserstein distance order p x y.

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
code: M1 <- matrix(rnorm(50), ncol = 5) M2 <- matrix(rnorm(250), ncol = 5) sliced_wasserstein(M1, M2) # random projection directions #> [1] 0.6387425 cardinal_axes <- diag(1, 5) sliced_wasserstein(M1, M2, thetas = cardinal_axes) #> [1] 0.5929873 first_two_axes <- cardinal_axes[1:2, ] sliced_wasserstein(M1, M2, thetas = first_two_axes) #> [1] 0.4632539
